title: Warcraft Lost America In One Weekend. China Turned It Into A $439 Million Movie.
dek: Five movies that flopped (or barely survived) at the U.S. box office — and then went on to make hundreds of millions more once they crossed the Pacific.

items.heading: Warcraft Bombed At Home, Then China Handed It $220 Million
items.text: Warcraft limped to a final domestic total of just $47.4 million against a $160 million budget, the kind of number that usually ends a franchise before it starts. But in China — where the game had a massive built-in fanbase — the movie exploded, eventually pulling in $220.8 million and pushing the worldwide total to $439 million, briefly making it the highest-grossing video game movie ever made.

images.caption: Warcraft: a $47M domestic dud that became a $439M global hit
Travis Fimmel led a cast most of America skipped — but China didn't

items.heading: Pacific Rim Barely Cracked $100M Domestic — China Made It A $411 Million Franchise
items.text: Guillermo del Toro's giant-robots-vs-monsters epic opened in third place behind Grown Ups 2 and Despicable Me 2, and finished with a merely okay $101.8 million domestically on a $190 million budget. Then China stepped in, delivering $111.9 million — more than the entire North American run — and helping push the worldwide total to $411 million, which is exactly why a sequel got greenlit at all.

images.caption: Pacific Rim: saved by the country that made up its biggest market
Idris Elba starred in a movie America shrugged at and China loved

items.heading: Terminator Genisys Was A Critical Disaster — Then China Made It A $440 Million Movie
items.text: Panned by critics and ignored by American audiences, Terminator Genisys ended its U.S. run with just $89.7 million. Then it hit Chinese theaters two months later during a Hollywood blackout window and stormed to $82.8 million in just eight days, nearly matching its entire domestic haul, on its way to becoming the first Hollywood release to top $100 million in China while making less than that in the U.S.

images.caption: Terminator Genisys: a domestic flop rescued almost entirely by China
Arnold Schwarzenegger's box office pull barely worked at home — but it worked overseas

items.heading: Nobody Expected The Meg To Work — China Made Sure It Did Anyway
items.text: Tracking had The Meg pegged for a modest $20-25 million domestic opening, and it ultimately closed out its U.S. run with $145.4 million. That would've been fine on its own, except international audiences — led by China, where the film was a co-production — added another $383.8 million, ballooning the worldwide total to $529 million and greenlighting an entire franchise built on a killer shark and Jason Statham punching things.

images.caption: The Meg: modest at home, massive everywhere else
Jason Statham's biggest global earner outside Fast & Furious

items.heading: Now You See Me 2 Made Just $65 Million At Home — Then China Doubled Down
items.text: Now You See Me 2 finished third at the domestic box office behind The Conjuring 2 and Warcraft, closing out its U.S. run with about $65 million. But thanks to scenes shot in Macau and a Mandarin theme song from Jay Chou, China alone delivered $97.1 million — the film's single biggest market — helping the sequel reach $335 million worldwide despite the shrug it got at home.

images.caption: Now You See Me 2: a domestic third-place finish that still made $335M globally
Mark Ruffalo returned for a sequel America barely noticed — but China did
